item.title: Jared Kushner’s Role as ‘Special Peace Envoy’ Is a 5-Alarm Constitutional Fire. Here's Why
item.description: In 2024, when pressed by a reporter, Jared Kushner ruled out joining Donald Trump’s second administration. “I’ve been very clear that my desire at this phase of my life is to focus on my firm,” he said , referring to Affinity Partners, the private equity company he started in 2021. Less than two…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
In 2024, when pressed by a reporter, Jared Kushner ruled out joining Donald Trump’s second administration.

“I’ve been very clear that my desire at this phase of my life is to focus on my firm,” he said, referring to Affinity Partners, the private equity company he started in 2021.

Less than two years later, he was back in his father-in-law’s administration, and while he has helped lead some of the most high-stakes foreign policy negotiations of the last year, he might have been telling the truth about his “focus” remaining on his firm.

As Kushner claims to be representing the United States in negotiations with Iran over a war his father-in-law started with the family’s lifelong personal friend, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, the 45-year-old is apparently planning to hit up Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and the United Arab Emirates – all foreign governments that depend on the US military for security and weaponry – for billions in funding for his private equity firm while already managing some of their investments. Even Iran’s foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi, sounded the alarm, posting a screenshot on X of the New York Times headline, “Jared Kushner Solicits Funds for His Firm While Working as Mideast Envoy.”

The questions too few people are asking are: Who is Kushner actually working for, anyway? And is he breaking the law in his role in his father-in-law’s administration?

Because the framers of the Constitution clearly envisioned that those who exercise significant public power would answer to the voters, Article II mandates Senate confirmation of “officers” of the United States. Although not a single person has cast a vote for Kushner, he is exercising a gross amount of federal authority as a private person. He was never confirmed by the Senate, so he cannot be constitutionally impeached. Heck, he’s not even on the government payroll. That’s allowed him to completely bypass the statutory and regulatory oversight laws binding federal employees, while the supine Congress looks the other way.

This is all while Kushner amasses extreme wealth by leveraging his father-in-law’s position of unparalleled global power to his own personal ends.

It’s a five-alarm constitutional fire. Grift aside, the fact that Kushner is playing a leading role in what is the most sensitive national security issue for the country right now – the disastrous war that Donald J. Trump recklessly started alongside Israel in Iran – without being bound by the ethics and transparency rules designed to protect the public interest, is an egregious affront to the Constitution, with deeply troubling implications for the nation. And people are barely even talking about it.

To understand just how corrupt and problematic Kushner’s role in government has become, we have to go back to Trump 1.0:

From 2017 until the end of Trump’s first term, Kushner operated as a “Senior Advisor to the President” along with his wife, Trump’s daughter, who was also an adviser. He was also the director of a new “Office of American Innovation,” which Kushner claimed would “bring a creative and strategic approach to many critical issues and intractable problems that affect Americans’ quality of life.” Kushner played key roles in Middle East policy, the COVID-19 response, criminal justice reform, and presidential pardons. His official claim to power? He was married to Trump’s daughter, Ivanka. Kushner never drew a government salary. Nor did he divest or put his extensive business assets in a blind trust, leaving in place what ethicists called “a web of conflicts of interest.”

Throughout Trump’s first term, Kushner stayed in close private contact with Saudi Prince Mohammed bin Salman, a coziness that prompted three senior US officials to question whether he was “susceptible to Saudi manipulation.” According to the Washington Post, “[former] Secretary of State Rex Tillerson and national security adviser H.R. McMaster expressed early concern that Kushner was freelancing US foreign policy.” McMaster was additionally “concerned there were no official records kept of what was said on the calls,” as “Kushner had contacts with foreign officials that he did not coordinate through the National Security Council or officially report.”

After Trump left the White House, Kushner received $2 billion from the Saudi Public Investment Fund controlled by MBS for his newly minted private equity firm, Affinity Partners, despite having no experience in private equity. That was just a drop in the bucket, however. According to an investigation by Democrats on the Senate Finance Committee and House Oversight Committee, “since 2021, Mr. Kushner has collected more than $110 million from the government of Saudi Arabia for investment management services that have reaped little to no return.”

In 2019, two White House security specialists rejected Jared Kushner’s bid for a top-secret clearance, calling his application “unfavorable.”Higher-ups overruled that decision. Kushner later confessed that he failed to mention four meetings with Russian officials during and after the campaign.”

Although incorporating presidential family members into the work of the White House is not new – presidents from James Madison to Franklin Roosevelt to Dwight Eisenhower have done it – the glaring scope of the current first family’s apparent corruption is unquestionably without precedent. When President Bill Clinton asked Hillary Clinton to lead a healthcare task force, a scandal erupted over whether a first lady could be considered a government employee; ultimately the courts allowed her to proceed. President John F. Kennedy’s nomination of his brother Robert Kennedy for attorney general led Congress to enact an anti-nepotism law in 1967.

Kushner’s outsized role during Trump 1.0 across a number of important governmental initiatives clearly ran afoul of the anti-nepotism statute, which bars public officials from appointing, employing, promoting, or advocating for their relatives’ advancement in civilian positions they serve in or control. That law expressly applies to presidents and makes clear that sons-in-law count as family members. But it has been completely useless as a bulwark against Trumpian nepotism because the penalty for non-compliance is reimbursement of the salary paid to the federal treasury, which Kushner didn’t bother to collect.

That’s not to mention that Trump’s Justice Department, on Jan. 20, 2017, broke decades of precedent to release an opinion conveniently concluding that the president is exempt from the anti-nepotism ban when hiring White House employees. It cited a different law giving presidents special authority to hire White House staff “without regard to any other provision of law regulating the employment or compensation of persons in the Government service” – effectively deadening the law specifically designed to prevent a repeat of the RFK situation.

In 2022, House Democrats launched an investigation into Kushner’s glaring conflicts, but Affinity Partners refused to cooperate. The investigation went nowhere.

The first administration was bad. But the twisted legal distortions behind Kushner’s unique position in the history of American government have gotten completely out of control since Trump retook office last year.

Although Kushner’s main job title in the current White House is still “son-in-law,” in February 2026, President Trump formalized the ad hoc role he had already been playing, referring to Kushner in official correspondence as “Special Envoy for Peace” and granting him a key role in Gaza and Ukraine talks along with another “special envoy,” Steve Witkoff, who likewise lacks any actual legal authority to act for the US. Both were also given executive seats on Trump’s so-called “Board of Peace” for Gaza. Even as a special envoy, Kushner is supposed to file a financial disclosure within 30 days of his appointment. Unlike Witkoff, he hasn’t done it.

Kushner is also operating in violation of longstanding federal ethics laws. Back in 1958, Congress imposed a “Code of Ethics for Government Service” requiring federal government officials and employees, when taking official action, to “put loyalty to the highest moral principles and to country above loyalty to persons, party, or Government department.” It also mandates that employees “[n]ever discriminate unfairly by the dispensing of special favors or privileges to anyone, whether for remuneration or not; and never accept for himself or his family, favors or benefits under circumstances which might be construed by reasonable persons as influencing the performance of his governmental duties.” It orders them to “[m]ake no private promises of any kind binding upon the duties of office, since a Government employee has no private word which can be binding on public duty;” to “[e]ngage in no business with the Government, either directly or indirectly which is inconsistent with the conscientious performance of his governmental duties;” to “[n]ever use any information coming to him confidentially in the performance of governmental duties as a means for making private profit;” to “[e]xpose corruption wherever discovered;” and to “[u]phold these principles, ever conscious that public office is a public trust.”

Kushner has comfortably exempted himself from all of this, stating: “Technically, I have not joined the administration, so I’m still just a volunteer like other businessmen who volunteer to help the government when asked.”

Last year, appearing on ‘60 Minutes’ alongside Steve Witkoff after the pair had negotiated the so-called “ceasefire” in Gaza (which turned out to be a ceasefire in name only), Kushner said, “What people call conflicts of interests, Steve and I call experience and trusted relationships that we have throughout the world.”

Democratic Rep. Jamie Raskin, who is now investigating Trump’s son-in-law’s potential conflicts of interest, asked Kushner about the implications of his “volunteerism” in an April 16 letter:

“When you approach negotiations related to the catastrophic Iran War, the prospect of prolonged military conflict there, the rights of women and religious minorities in the Middle East or the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, are you representing 100% the interests of your business partners in Saudi Arabia and other Gulf oil autocracies or are you representing 100% the interests of the American people? Or is it a 50%-50% split that you apportion in your loyalties between your official and corporate clients? Your client Saudi Arabia wants to see a continuation and escalation of President Trump’s Iran war, but the American people have an interest in minimizing the loss of American lives and treasure in this conflict. To whom do your professional obligations and fiduciary duties belong?”

Kushner’s glib response? That Affinity Partners has stated that it and Kushner have
”abided by all laws and regulations and will continue to do so.”

Not to worry, folks. Kushner’s private equity team – while it solicits billions from the Saudi government – is self-policing for compliance with the laws that apply to everyone else in government. The difference is that everyone else is banned from profiting off their federal positions. Affinity Partners is not. So it’s doing both. Kusher is no peace envoy – he’s an embodied threat to democracy itself, with the blood and treasure of the people in his hands.

Kimberly Wehle, Zeteo’s legal contributor and author of ‘ Constitution in Crisis,’ is a constitutional law professor at the University of Baltimore School of Law.

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: BREAKING: Tennessee State Republicans Are Voting to Eliminate Only Black-Majority District
item.description: Tennessee Republicans are set to redraw and eliminate the only majority-Black congressional district in the state today. State Rep. Justin Pearson – a member of the infamous “Tennessee Three” representatives who demonstrated in the state capitol after the 2023 Covenant School shooting – joined…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Tennessee Republicans are set to redraw and eliminate the only majority-Black congressional district in the state today.

State Rep. Justin Pearson – a member of the infamous “Tennessee Three” representatives who demonstrated in the state capitol after the 2023 Covenant School shooting – joined Zeteo on Wednesday night to explain exactly what is going on.

After the far-right Supreme Court issued a regressive ruling weakening the Voting Rights Act, Republican Governor Bill Lee quickly arranged a special legislative session to set about redistricting the state, to take the already 8-1 balance in favor of Republicans to 9-0.

Pearson himself is a congressional candidate for the Democratic district that Republicans are seeking to eliminate.

Speaking with Prem, State Rep. Pearson details the historical legacy of racist voter suppression in the South, lays out the dire stakes at hand for both Tennessee and the nation, and reflects on how Democrats failed to prevent this moment and what needs to be done now to stop it.

“…if we don’t get power and use it for the benefit of liberation for everybody, we don’t deserve it. And I think in this moment, it is forcing us to ask ourselves tough questions,” Pearson says. “What have we done with the power that we have? And what more can we do while we still got it?”

He also looks back to heroic figures of history, who help guide him in this moment.

“Those folks, when they did that, they never knew my name, but they did it anyway. And what they carried in their mind and what they carried in their heart was that this country could be different and that it would require some sacrifice. It would require some suffering,” Pearson says. “And here I am an inheritor of that legacy. And I have a responsibility to use the position that I’m in, the platform that I have, to fight for the kids whose names I will never know.”

We have removed the paywall from this timely interview. Please subscribe to Zeteo to help us do more of this work that the mainstream media fails to.

Watch the video above to hear from Pearson directly from the thick of the legislative session Republicans expeditiously threw together to dilute Black political power in the South.

Check out more from Zeteo:

Thank you for being a paid subscriber to Zeteo, your support helps keep this media movement alive. If you’d like to wear your support, don’t forget to check out

For any issues with your subscription, please email us at info@zeteonews.com.

item.title: Trump’s America Is Not a Serious Country – But We Are Seriously Screwed
item.description: On this day in 1994, the Norwegian painter Edvard Munch’s masterpiece, The Scream, was recovered almost three months after being stolen from the National Gallery in Oslo. An enduring symbol of existential angst – perhaps never more relevant than now – the painting would likely sell for hundreds of…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1994, the Norwegian painter Edvard Munch’s masterpiece, The Scream, was recovered almost three months after being stolen from the National Gallery in Oslo. An enduring symbol of existential angst – perhaps never more relevant than now – the painting would likely sell for hundreds of millions of dollars.

Good morning… Martin here, delighted to be writing ‘First Draft’ again. Wednesday was another utterly ridiculous day in Donald Trump’s America, from alarming lows to social media sugar highs and back again, the WWE Smackdown presidency in full effect even while our freedoms are attacked and a major Middle East war careens from one lethal threat to another.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ the president talks UFC while the press tries to talk Iran, the FBI threatens journalists and Democrats … and a private prison company cashes in on Trump’s ICE terror. Welcome to America.

Two things can be true at the same time. Under the presidency of Donald Trump, the US has ceased to be a serious country. Under the presidency of Donald Trump, the US is seriously screwed.

On Wednesday, Trump welcomed reporters into the Oval Office. The US-Israel war with Iran remained unresolved. A cost-of-living crisis, supercharged by that war and its effect on world oil prices, surged ever onwards. With Trump’s polls in the toilet, his party was racing to disenfranchise Black voters, as enabled by the Supreme Court.

That wasn’t all there was for the president to discuss. His administration had just unveiled a new counterterrorism strategy, while the FBI was reportedly investigating both a reporter who filed an unflattering piece about its director and a Virginia Democrat working to counter Republican electoral shenanigans.

The country was on fire. Trump wanted to talk mixed martial arts.

Surrounded by professional fighters, the president, in his baggy suit and Freudian tie, played ringmaster for his next White House desecration: a UFC event, set for the night of June 14, to celebrate America’s 250th birthday and Trump’s 80th, not necessarily in that order.

“We’re having a big fight,” Trump said. “It’s never going to happen again, never happened before, and it’s all of the best fighters, best four fighters standing right behind me and all champions and it’s going to happen right in front of the White House, here’s a picture.”

He showed a mock-up. He talked, and bragged, and when they could get a word in, the fighters talked and bragged. Eventually, Trump asked for questions, though “only for the fighters,” because the reporters would “want to ask about Iran and a couple other things.”

Among the would-be Woodwards and Bernsteins, one fearless soul spoke up.

“I’ll bridge this, here you go. You’re basically – wait, wait, this will work. You’re facing an opponent right now in Iran that has refused to submit. You seem optimistic now that you may be closer to a deal. What’s different about this moment now than in other moments where a deal has seemed close?”

And we were off, into an impromptu press conference, the president discussing a major war while surrounded by professional brawlers. (On Tuesday, it was kids. It was hard to tell which group seemed more nonplussed.) UFC is not WWE – the punches and kicks actually land – but the “ kayfabe presidency” sprang to mind once again.

There’s no escaping it: Trump and his administration are fundamentally unserious people who pose a fundamentally serious threat to the rule of law in the United States, to American democracy, and therefore to world peace.

Take the FBI.

On Tuesday, MS Now reported that the bureau is investigating Sarah Fitzpatrick, a reporter whose exposé on the bizarre reign of FBI director-cum-Trump goon Kash Patel in The Atlantic prompted amazement, fierce denials, and a $250 million defamation lawsuit from Patel, widely seen as more of a way to keep his job a bit longer than a serious legal challenge.

The FBI denied it was investigating Fitzpatrick, but the chilling nature of the report was clear. The Atlantic editor Jeffrey Goldberg warned of “an outrageous, illegal, and dangerous attack on the free press and the First Amendment.”

Elsewhere, Fox first reported an FBI raid on the office of Virginia Senate President Pro Tempore L. Louise Lucas, who played a key role in passing a Democratic gerrymander to counter Republican gerrymanders elsewhere. Other outlets confirmed a long-running investigation into alleged corruption, originating under Joe Biden. But the timing of the raid seemed indicative of a central feature of Trump’s regime: federal law enforcement wielded against political foes. Readers were reminded of the New York Times reporter who wrote about Patel’s girlfriend, only to be investigated for stalking. They were reminded of former FBI director James Comey, indicted last week for threatening the president … with a picture of some seashells.

And then, with impeccable timing, Fitzpatrick returned to The Atlantic with a story about how Patel, whose alleged heavy drinking featured in her first report, often travels “with a supply of personalized branded bourbon,” bottles of Woodford Reserve that say “Kash Patel FBI Director” on them, which he gives as gifts.

Fitzpatrick said one “former longtime senior FBI official … burst out laughing,” when asked if any other FBI director had given out personally branded booze. The internet burst out laughing too. But Fitzpatrick also quoted George Hill, a former FBI supervisory intelligence analyst, who said: “Handing out bottles of liquor at the premier law-enforcement agency – it makes me frightened for the country. Standards apply to everything and everyone – especially the boss.”

“When you degrade the office like that, you degrade the impact,” Hill noted.

Unserious, serious. Serious, unserious. And so the show rolled on.

Sebastian Gorka is an unserious man who takes himself very seriously indeed: a British-schooled booming voice (with alleged sartorial links to Hungarian fascists) who, after losing his place in the first Trump administration, took to selling fish oil and warning about krakens, but who is now back, as senior director for counterterrorism.

On Tuesday, Gorka unveiled a new counterterrorism strategy that wielded ideology over evidence, naming “violent left-wing extremists” and “extremist transgender ideology” among primary threats to the nation.

“We see a threat, we will respond to it, and we will crush it, whether it is the cartels, the jihadists, or violent left-wing extremists like antifa and like the transgender killers,” Gorka told reporters.

Under Biden, in 2023, white supremacy was named the “most dangerous terrorist threat” to US security. Not anymore, it’s not.

For good measure, Gorka also told reporters that suggesting the US should not have attacked Iran “means you are testicularly challenged.”

Unseriously serious. Seriously unserious. We’re all in serious trouble.

Death tolls:

  • Iran:Iranian state media on Sundayreported, citing officials, that US-Israeli attacks have killed 3,468 people.
  • Lebanon:Israel has killed more than 2,715 people, including at least 177 children, in Lebanon, according to thecountry’s Health Ministry.
  • US troops:At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.
  • Israel:At least35people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed, according to officials.

And in more war news…

  • Talks talk:The US and Iran differed on how close an end to the war might be. Trumptoldreporters, “We’re in good shape, and now we’re doing well,” but also said, “If they don’t agree, the bombing starts.” Tehranrejecteda report that a memorandum to end the war was close.
  • War prep:An Israeli sourcetoldReuters the country was not aware of any imminent peace deal and was preparing for more fighting. Benjamin Netanyahu said he was due to talk to Trump and wanted Iran to lose all nuclear capabilities. Trump wasreportedto see complete nuclear surrender as a “red line.”
  • Damage report:Accordingto theWashington Post, US sites have sustained more damage than reported, with 228 Iranian hits.
  • Angry allies:Multiple outletsreportedthat Trump’s abrupt abandonment of “Project Freedom,” his plan to move vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, was not strategic but forced by allies, including Saudi Arabia and Kuwait,refusingto let the US use bases and airspace.
  • Stark warning:Oil prices dropped, but a Carlyle Group economistsaidEuropean storage would run dry this month, while US tanks would empty around July 4, with three months needed to restart flows even if the Strait opened tomorrow.
  • Crisis? What crisis?Trumpsaidhe was surprised oil prices hadn’t gone higher, “but even if it went to $200 [a barrel], it would have been worth it.” Adviser Kevin Hassettsaidyes, gas prices were up, but “credit card spending is through the roof,” which he apparently saw as a good thing, not an indication of spiraling debt.
  • Beirut strike:Israelstruckthe Lebanese capital for the first time since the supposed ceasefire, since which it has continued to attack the south of the country. Israel said it killed a senior Hezbollah commander.

Private prison company GEO Group, one of the largest contractors for Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), nearly doubled profits in the first quarter of 2026 after achieving record sales last year, thanks to President Donald Trump’s lawless war on immigrants.

The company’s latest earnings report, released Wednesday, said net income increased year-over-year by a whopping 96% in the first quarter, and total revenue reached $705.2 million.

The company said earlier this year in its 2025 report that it saw the “largest amount of new business… won in a single year,” with new or expanded contracts totaling $520 million. Most of that – about $400 million – came from contracts for five ICE detention facilities.

GEO Group’s soaring profits come after the company’s PAC bankrolled Trump’s 2024 presidential campaign, which he centered on anti-immigrant rhetoric.

The company’s corporate PAC was the first to max out contributions to Trump in 2024, according to an analysis by Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington. Since last year, two company subsidiaries have contributed more than $2.8 million to Trump’s super PAC and outside groups backing congressional Republicans.

GEO Group did not respond to a request for comment.

In a filmed deposition in 2022, Donald Trump thought a picture of E. Jean Carroll, the writer for whose sexual abuse and defamation he was eventually found liable, was a picture of which of his three wives?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

Rutgers University in New Jersey has canceled a graduation speech scheduled to be delivered by Rami Elghandour, an alum, biotech CEO, and executive producer of the Gaza documentary ‘The Voice of Hind Rajab.’

Rutgers invited Elghandour in December. But five months on, days before graduation, university administrators told him his speech had been canceled due to an unknown number of students complaining about unidentified social media posts.

In his first video interview on the issue, Elghandour took Zeteo inside his cancellation.

  • Tweet delete:Trump’s pick for surgeon general, Nicole Saphier, deleted tweets criticizing the president and Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., CNNsaid.
  • Uncivil action:The Justice Department willaskthe Supreme Court to let it intervene in Trump’s appeal of his $83.3 million penalty in E. Jean Carroll’s defamation suit, and thereby let him off.
  • Gun suit:The DOJsuedColorado over a law banning high-capacity ammunition magazines, passed after the 2012 Aurora movie theater shooting, in which 12 people were killed.
  • No chance:The Supreme Courtdeniedan attempt to recall its judgment inLouisiana v. Callais, which gutted the Voting Rights Act and sent Republican states rushing to take away Black Americans’ voting power. Alabamavoted to do soeven as tornado warnings sounded and the state capitol went dark. Meanwhile, Chief Justice John Robertsbleatedabout people seeing justices as “political actors.”
  • Epstein latest:Howard Lutnick met in private with the House Oversight Committee about his links to the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. Democratsquestionedthe commerce secretary’s changing story and claimed memory lapses. TheNew York Timespublisheda purported suicide note by Epstein.
  • Debt push:The Trump administration is using debt collectors with records of abusive practices to hound immigrants over multi-million-dollar fines for failing to leave the US, The Leverscooped.
  • Serious polling:YouGov said 55% of Americansthinkthey could beat Trump in a fight, though more Republicans (39%) think they would lose to theoverweight,sleepy79-year-old than think they’d beat him (33%). CNNsaidmore Americans believe in ghosts and telepathy than support Trump’s White House ballroom.
  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israel killed at least three Palestinians, including a child, as they constructed a toilet in Gaza’s Zeitoun neighborhood today. Another Palestinian was killed in an Israeli drone strike,Al Jazeera reported. Palestinians officials also announced that the son of the head of Hamas’s political bureau died after being injured in an Israeli strike on Wednesday.
  • 🇺🇦🇭🇺 Gold returned:Ukrainian President Volodymyr ZelenskysaidHungary had given back cash and gold worth around $82 million, seized earlier this year. Such moves have followed the removal of pro-Russian Hungarian PM Viktor Orbán.

  • 🇨🇺🇵🇦

    Americas aims:Responding to Secretary of State Marco Rubio saying the status quo in Cuba was “unacceptable,” Foreign Minister Bruno RodriguezsaidUS threats to attack were “international crimes.” Bloombergreportedthat US troops are training in Panama and eyeing other Latin American countries.

  • 🇨🇻

    Cruise moves:Two infected passengers and one suspected case were evacuated from a cruise ship stricken with hantavirus before it left Cape Verde for the Canary Islands, officials said. Three people have died in the outbreak.


Donald Trump thinks there’s a thing called “spell correct” or “word correct” on his phone, and it was part of the great media conspiracy against him because it changed “Melania” to “Melody” when he posted about his wife. And he says he got “the military” to fix the problem. So … that happened.

Zeteo’s Minnah Arshad and Prem Thakker contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Trump’s America Is Not a Serious Country – But We Are Seriously Screwed
item.description: On this day in 1994, the Norwegian painter Edvard Munch’s masterpiece, The Scream, was recovered almost three months after being stolen from the National Gallery in Oslo. An enduring symbol of existential angst – perhaps never more relevant than now – the painting would likely sell for hundreds of…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1994, the Norwegian painter Edvard Munch’s masterpiece, The Scream, was recovered almost three months after being stolen from the National Gallery in Oslo. An enduring symbol of existential angst – perhaps never more relevant than now – the painting would likely sell for hundreds of millions of dollars.

Good morning… Martin here, delighted to be writing ‘First Draft’ again. Wednesday was another utterly ridiculous day in Donald Trump’s America, from alarming lows to social media sugar highs and back again, the WWE Smackdown presidency in full effect even while our freedoms are attacked and a major Middle East war careens from one lethal threat to another.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ the president talks UFC while the press tries to talk Iran, the FBI threatens journalists and Democrats … and a private prison company cashes in on Trump’s ICE terror. Welcome to America.

Two things can be true at the same time. Under the presidency of Donald Trump, the US has ceased to be a serious country. Under the presidency of Donald Trump, the US is seriously screwed.

On Wednesday, Trump welcomed reporters into the Oval Office. The US-Israel war with Iran remained unresolved. A cost-of-living crisis, supercharged by that war and its effect on world oil prices, surged ever onwards. With Trump’s polls in the toilet, his party was racing to disenfranchise Black voters, as enabled by the Supreme Court.

That wasn’t all there was for the president to discuss. His administration had just unveiled a new counterterrorism strategy, while the FBI was reportedly investigating both a reporter who filed an unflattering piece about its director and a Virginia Democrat working to counter Republican electoral shenanigans.

The country was on fire. Trump wanted to talk mixed martial arts.

Surrounded by professional fighters, the president, in his baggy suit and Freudian tie, played ringmaster for his next White House desecration: a UFC event, set for the night of June 14, to celebrate America’s 250th birthday and Trump’s 80th, not necessarily in that order.

“We’re having a big fight,” Trump said. “It’s never going to happen again, never happened before, and it’s all of the best fighters, best four fighters standing right behind me and all champions and it’s going to happen right in front of the White House, here’s a picture.”

He showed a mock-up. He talked, and bragged, and when they could get a word in, the fighters talked and bragged. Eventually, Trump asked for questions, though “only for the fighters,” because the reporters would “want to ask about Iran and a couple other things.”

Among the would-be Woodwards and Bernsteins, one fearless soul spoke up.

“I’ll bridge this, here you go. You’re basically – wait, wait, this will work. You’re facing an opponent right now in Iran that has refused to submit. You seem optimistic now that you may be closer to a deal. What’s different about this moment now than in other moments where a deal has seemed close?”

And we were off, into an impromptu press conference, the president discussing a major war while surrounded by professional brawlers. (On Tuesday, it was kids. It was hard to tell which group seemed more nonplussed.) UFC is not WWE – the punches and kicks actually land – but the “ kayfabe presidency” sprang to mind once again.

There’s no escaping it: Trump and his administration are fundamentally unserious people who pose a fundamentally serious threat to the rule of law in the United States, to American democracy, and therefore to world peace.

Take the FBI.

On Tuesday, MS Now reported that the bureau is investigating Sarah Fitzpatrick, a reporter whose exposé on the bizarre reign of FBI director-cum-Trump goon Kash Patel in The Atlantic prompted amazement, fierce denials, and a $250 million defamation lawsuit from Patel, widely seen as more of a way to keep his job a bit longer than a serious legal challenge.

The FBI denied it was investigating Fitzpatrick, but the chilling nature of the report was clear. The Atlantic editor Jeffrey Goldberg warned of “an outrageous, illegal, and dangerous attack on the free press and the First Amendment.”

Elsewhere, Fox first reported an FBI raid on the office of Virginia Senate President Pro Tempore L. Louise Lucas, who played a key role in passing a Democratic gerrymander to counter Republican gerrymanders elsewhere. Other outlets confirmed a long-running investigation into alleged corruption, originating under Joe Biden. But the timing of the raid seemed indicative of a central feature of Trump’s regime: federal law enforcement wielded against political foes. Readers were reminded of the New York Times reporter who wrote about Patel’s girlfriend, only to be investigated for stalking. They were reminded of former FBI director James Comey, indicted last week for threatening the president … with a picture of some seashells.

And then, with impeccable timing, Fitzpatrick returned to The Atlantic with a story about how Patel, whose alleged heavy drinking featured in her first report, often travels “with a supply of personalized branded bourbon,” bottles of Woodford Reserve that say “Kash Patel FBI Director” on them, which he gives as gifts.

Fitzpatrick said one “former longtime senior FBI official … burst out laughing,” when asked if any other FBI director had given out personally branded booze. The internet burst out laughing too. But Fitzpatrick also quoted George Hill, a former FBI supervisory intelligence analyst, who said: “Handing out bottles of liquor at the premier law-enforcement agency – it makes me frightened for the country. Standards apply to everything and everyone – especially the boss.”

“When you degrade the office like that, you degrade the impact,” Hill noted.

Unserious, serious. Serious, unserious. And so the show rolled on.

Sebastian Gorka is an unserious man who takes himself very seriously indeed: a British-schooled booming voice (with alleged sartorial links to Hungarian fascists) who, after losing his place in the first Trump administration, took to selling fish oil and warning about krakens, but who is now back, as senior director for counterterrorism.

On Tuesday, Gorka unveiled a new counterterrorism strategy that wielded ideology over evidence, naming “violent left-wing extremists” and “extremist transgender ideology” among primary threats to the nation.

“We see a threat, we will respond to it, and we will crush it, whether it is the cartels, the jihadists, or violent left-wing extremists like antifa and like the transgender killers,” Gorka told reporters.

Under Biden, in 2023, white supremacy was named the “most dangerous terrorist threat” to US security. Not anymore, it’s not.

For good measure, Gorka also told reporters that suggesting the US should not have attacked Iran “means you are testicularly challenged.”

Unseriously serious. Seriously unserious. We’re all in serious trouble.

Death tolls:

  • Iran:Iranian state media on Sundayreported, citing officials, that US-Israeli attacks have killed 3,468 people.
  • Lebanon:Israel has killed more than 2,715 people, including at least 177 children, in Lebanon, according to thecountry’s Health Ministry.
  • US troops:At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.
  • Israel:At least35people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed, according to officials.

And in more war news…

  • Talks talk:The US and Iran differed on how close an end to the war might be. Trumptoldreporters, “We’re in good shape, and now we’re doing well,” but also said, “If they don’t agree, the bombing starts.” Tehranrejecteda report that a memorandum to end the war was close.
  • War prep:An Israeli sourcetoldReuters the country was not aware of any imminent peace deal and was preparing for more fighting. Benjamin Netanyahu said he was due to talk to Trump and wanted Iran to lose all nuclear capabilities. Trump wasreportedto see complete nuclear surrender as a “red line.”
  • Damage report:Accordingto theWashington Post, US sites have sustained more damage than reported, with 228 Iranian hits.
  • Angry allies:Multiple outletsreportedthat Trump’s abrupt abandonment of “Project Freedom,” his plan to move vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, was not strategic but forced by allies, including Saudi Arabia and Kuwait,refusingto let the US use bases and airspace.
  • Stark warning:Oil prices dropped, but a Carlyle Group economistsaidEuropean storage would run dry this month, while US tanks would empty around July 4, with three months needed to restart flows even if the Strait opened tomorrow.
  • Crisis? What crisis?Trumpsaidhe was surprised oil prices hadn’t gone higher, “but even if it went to $200 [a barrel], it would have been worth it.” Adviser Kevin Hassettsaidyes, gas prices were up, but “credit card spending is through the roof,” which he apparently saw as a good thing, not an indication of spiraling debt.
  • Beirut strike:Israelstruckthe Lebanese capital for the first time since the supposed ceasefire, since which it has continued to attack the south of the country. Israel said it killed a senior Hezbollah commander.

Private prison company GEO Group, one of the largest contractors for Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), nearly doubled profits in the first quarter of 2026 after achieving record sales last year, thanks to President Donald Trump’s lawless war on immigrants.

The company’s latest earnings report, released Wednesday, said net income increased year-over-year by a whopping 96% in the first quarter, and total revenue reached $705.2 million.

The company said earlier this year in its 2025 report that it saw the “largest amount of new business… won in a single year,” with new or expanded contracts totaling $520 million. Most of that – about $400 million – came from contracts for five ICE detention facilities.

GEO Group’s soaring profits come after the company’s PAC bankrolled Trump’s 2024 presidential campaign, which he centered on anti-immigrant rhetoric.

The company’s corporate PAC was the first to max out contributions to Trump in 2024, according to an analysis by Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington. Since last year, two company subsidiaries have contributed more than $2.8 million to Trump’s super PAC and outside groups backing congressional Republicans.

GEO Group did not respond to a request for comment.

In a filmed deposition in 2022, Donald Trump thought a picture of E. Jean Carroll, the writer for whose sexual abuse and defamation he was eventually found liable, was a picture of which of his three wives?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

Rutgers University in New Jersey has canceled a graduation speech scheduled to be delivered by Rami Elghandour, an alum, biotech CEO, and executive producer of the Gaza documentary ‘The Voice of Hind Rajab.’

Rutgers invited Elghandour in December. But five months on, days before graduation, university administrators told him his speech had been canceled due to an unknown number of students complaining about unidentified social media posts.

In his first video interview on the issue, Elghandour took Zeteo inside his cancellation.

  • Tweet delete:Trump’s pick for surgeon general, Nicole Saphier, deleted tweets criticizing the president and Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., CNNsaid.
  • Uncivil action:The Justice Department willaskthe Supreme Court to let it intervene in Trump’s appeal of his $83.3 million penalty in E. Jean Carroll’s defamation suit, and thereby let him off.
  • Gun suit:The DOJsuedColorado over a law banning high-capacity ammunition magazines, passed after the 2012 Aurora movie theater shooting, in which 12 people were killed.
  • No chance:The Supreme Courtdeniedan attempt to recall its judgment inLouisiana v. Callais, which gutted the Voting Rights Act and sent Republican states rushing to take away Black Americans’ voting power. Alabamavoted to do soeven as tornado warnings sounded and the state capitol went dark. Meanwhile, Chief Justice John Robertsbleatedabout people seeing justices as “political actors.”
  • Epstein latest:Howard Lutnick met in private with the House Oversight Committee about his links to the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. Democratsquestionedthe commerce secretary’s changing story and claimed memory lapses. TheNew York Timespublisheda purported suicide note by Epstein.
  • Debt push:The Trump administration is using debt collectors with records of abusive practices to hound immigrants over multi-million-dollar fines for failing to leave the US, The Leverscooped.
  • Serious polling:YouGov said 55% of Americansthinkthey could beat Trump in a fight, though more Republicans (39%) think they would lose to theoverweight,sleepy79-year-old than think they’d beat him (33%). CNNsaidmore Americans believe in ghosts and telepathy than support Trump’s White House ballroom.
  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israel killed at least three Palestinians, including a child, as they constructed a toilet in Gaza’s Zeitoun neighborhood today. Another Palestinian was killed in an Israeli drone strike,Al Jazeera reported. Palestinians officials also announced that the son of the head of Hamas’s political bureau died after being injured in an Israeli strike on Wednesday.
  • 🇺🇦🇭🇺 Gold returned:Ukrainian President Volodymyr ZelenskysaidHungary had given back cash and gold worth around $82 million, seized earlier this year. Such moves have followed the removal of pro-Russian Hungarian PM Viktor Orbán.

  • 🇨🇺🇵🇦

    Americas aims:Responding to Secretary of State Marco Rubio saying the status quo in Cuba was “unacceptable,” Foreign Minister Bruno RodriguezsaidUS threats to attack were “international crimes.” Bloombergreportedthat US troops are training in Panama and eyeing other Latin American countries.

  • 🇨🇻

    Cruise moves:Two infected passengers and one suspected case were evacuated from a cruise ship stricken with hantavirus before it left Cape Verde for the Canary Islands, officials said. Three people have died in the outbreak.


Donald Trump thinks there’s a thing called “spell correct” or “word correct” on his phone, and it was part of the great media conspiracy against him because it changed “Melania” to “Melody” when he posted about his wife. And he says he got “the military” to fix the problem. So … that happened.

Zeteo’s Minnah Arshad and Prem Thakker contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

item.title: Bari Weiss ‘Meddles’ With ‘CBS Sunday Morning’ Story on Palestine
item.description: Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’ Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, I devote the bulk of the newsletter to a ‘CBS News Sunday Morning’ story about the West Bank that…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, I devote the bulk of the newsletter to a ‘CBS News Sunday Morning’ story about the West Bank that sparked outrage from one of its interview subjects – and how Bari Weiss fits in all of this. Also, Donald Trump weighs in on the passing of Ted Turner – and gets everything wrong.

“I am deeply troubled by my portrayal and the omission of key facts in the segment in which I participated in good faith,” Zaid Azhari, in a message reviewed by Zeteo, wrote to CBS News producer Sari Aviv last month.

A cultural heritage researcher and leader of the “Save Sebastia Campaign,” Azhari was upset over a ‘CBS News Sunday Morning’ story that aired on April 5 and centered on the political turmoil surrounding Israeli archeological digs in the occupied West Bank, asserting that his interview for the piece was “selectively edited to falsely portray me as someone who erases Jewish history.”

According to four sources, the report was subjected to last-minute edits and script changes by self-avowed “Zionist fanatic” Bari Weiss, the CBS editor-in-chief who has made sweeping changes at the network since her hiring last fall. The story had been in the works for months, and prior to Weiss joining CBS.

“We aim to inform audiences, not satisfy story subjects. That’s what this piece did. We encourage everyone to watch it and judge for themselves,” a CBS News spokesperson said in a statement.

“Oh yeah, she definitely meddled,” one CBS News senior staffer told me, while other sources noted that this represented a new front in Weiss’s editorial “interference” at the network.

“Bari found out about the story and made changes to the script. She has never jumped in editorially on a ‘Sunday Morning’ story until this one,” one CBS insider said, while a network reporter noted that “this piece is the first time she’s pulled like a CECOT on the show.”

In their comment to Zeteo, a CBS News spokesperson did not directly address the claims about Weiss’s involvement in the ‘Sunday Morning’ story.

The CECOT story, of course, is a reference to ‘60 Minutes’ correspondent Sharyn Alfonsi’s piece that Weiss abruptly pulled at the 11th hour because she felt it lacked “critical voices” from the Trump administration, prompting Alfonsi to decry the move as a “political decision.” (The segment eventually aired a month later.) Alfonsi, who recently bemoaned the “spread of corporate meddling and editorial fear” at the network, is expected to be let go after the venerable newsmagazine’s season ends.

Still, a ‘Sunday Morning’ staffer suggested to Zeteo that Weiss’s edits to the piece may not have substantively changed the tenor of the segment, adding that it was “problematic from the beginning” and that they weren’t sure if her involvement had much to do with how the final product turned out.

Indeed, the segment had been in the works since before Weiss officially joined CBS News as its top editor. Seth Doane, the veteran correspondent who reported the story, traveled to the West Bank and Israel in mid-September to interview Azhari and the other subjects.

Though ‘Sunday Morning’ has largely been associated with human-interest feature stories over the decades and has generally steered clear of politically charged and controversial topics, the program decided to take on the subject of tensions between Palestine and Israel. In this instance, it focused on the escalating disputes in the West Bank over archeological sites – which Palestinian residents have said are being “weaponized” by Israeli settlers to appropriate more land.

Throughout the 11-minute segment, Doane speaks with a number of archaeologists, residents, and government officials to understand how archaeology and historic artifacts have become so politically fraught in the area. It was his conversation with Azhari and how it was edited and framed that sparked a complaint.

Azhari told me his interview was edited in a way that portrayed him as a “stupid Palestinian who’s just a resident from Sebastia,” referring to one of the oldest continuously inhabited Palestinian towns in the West Bank and an area home to several ancient sites.

In the finished segment, most of Azhari’s remarks were contrasted with those of Amichai Eliyahu, the far-right Israeli minister of heritage who has called for the annexation of the entire West Bank and said that nuking Gaza was one option, suggesting that they represented the two extremes of the debate over the archeological digs. While the segment noted that Eliyahu is from a “far-right political faction,” the story did not mention his extremist positions or remarks.

After Eliyahu ridiculously tells Doane that the Palestinian people were “invented 60 years ago,” Doane notes in the report that it reminded him “of something we heard in Sebastia,” referencing his conversation with Azhari. Discussing a plaque in the city, Doane stated that it “made no mention that Sebastia had been the capital city of the Kingdom of Israel.”

This was followed by Doane telling Azhari that “on both sides, there’s an erasure of history” and that the plaque does “not include a Jewish history.” The response from Azhari that was aired in the piece, however, appeared to be cobbled together from different portions of the interview.

“To say this is a Jewish kingdom or a Jewish city, it’s not really that correct,” Azhari says in the segment. An additional comment, which showed him sitting in a different location, featured him stating, “This is my heritage. This is not the Zionist history or culture, this is mine.”

“This is mine. This is what we kept hearing on both sides,” Doane declared in a voiceover.

In his message to Aviv, Azhari wrote that it constituted “clear editorial malpractice” that his interview was held for seven months, only to air in “a selectively edited format” that reflected a “deliberate effort to construct a one-sided narrative.”

“This is exactly the mess they wanted from the beginning. Okay, I am from Sebastia. I am an activist, working on cultural heritage, but I am not a historical specialist,” Azhari told me in our interview.

“I am not an archaeologist. I am not someone who studied history. I am just like representing the local community of Sebastia, representing the organization that is working to develop tourism and cultural heritage,” he continued. “That’s my point. I talked about it from that way. But when you are hosting six Israelis, you need to host at least one Palestinian archeologist or the Ministry of Tourism, anyone of these people who can really tell the Palestinian opinion, or the Palestinian story about it.”

He called the editing of his interviews “deceptive” and underscored that it was “categorically untrue” that he wanted to erase Jewish history. Adding that he and the local community are “faithful custodians of all historical layers—Jewish/Iron Age, Roman, Christian, and Muslim alike,” Azhari wrote that he provided Doane “with the same historically grounded narrative we share with all visitors, yet the final edit suggests otherwise.”

Another problem Azhari raised in his email is that the piece suggested archaeology was “becoming” political. “In reality, archaeology has long been weaponized as a tool of state power,” he stated, adding that “the current settlement-driven interventions” represented an “act of segregation and erasure.”

Additionally, Azhari took issue with how others in the piece were portrayed, specifically Eliyahu and Preserving the Eternal, a settler lobbying organization described as merely “an Israeli archaeology group” in the piece. Emek Shaveh, which had two of its members interviewed for the story, was referred to as a “left-wing Israeli archeology group.”

“This is a serious failure of journalistic responsibility,” Azhari asserted in his email.

“My main mission is to have these sites safe, protected, and preserved. If it were to be done by the Palestinian Authority, fine. But unfortunately, they don’t do that,” Preserving the Eternal’s Adi Shragai said to Doane when asked if the excavations amounted to land grabs in the West Bank.

In the months since Doane trekked to Sebastia to interview Azhari, and prior to the story airing, the Israeli government announced its intention to seize about 450 acres of Sebastia, having already allocated millions of dollars to develop the hilltop archeological site. According to The Guardian, the “current plans for development of the site involve a visitors’ center, a car park, and a fence that will separate the ruins from the rest of the town.” This reality was not included in the CBS story.

The majority of residents in the town, meanwhile, depend on tourism at the ruins or the nearby olive orchards – which would be inaccessible once the site is developed – for their livelihoods. The expropriation would be Israel’s largest ever seizure of land for an archeological project. It is also expected that a large Jewish settlement will be expanded near the site.

“Unfortunately Sebastia has gone into a dark tunnel,” Sebastia Mayor Mahmud Azem said to The Guardian at the time. “It is an aggression against Palestinian landowners, against olive trees, against tourist sites and it is a violation of the history and the heritage of Palestine.”

Jasper Nathaniel, an independent Jewish journalist who has spent the past two years reporting on the “archeological warfare in the West Bank,” told me that he rejected the notion that Azhari or any other residents of Sebastia were looking to erase “Jewish history” from the historic site.

It’s “simply undeniable how much” Sebastia’s residents “treasure all of the ruins” and “really think of themselves as the custodians of the ruins,” he added. The occupying Israeli forces in the area, Nathaniel noted, have now made that impossible for them.

In our conversation, Azhari repeatedly pointed out that he provided all of this context to Doane throughout the interview, but none of it made it to air.

“The report just showed a Palestinian guy living in Sebastia having a very, very closed mentality, not believing there are others in this world, and the whole idea that Palestinians are not accepting of others,” he concluded.

Azhari has yet to receive a response to his complaint to CBS, which was sent over two weeks ago.

Ted Turner, the revolutionary founder of CNN and TBS who changed the face of television forever, died on Wednesday at the age of 87.

Amid the flood of tributes and obituaries lionizing the influential media mogul, lauding his philanthropy, conservationism, and “audacious vision” when it came to 24-hour cable news, Donald Trump naturally had to enter the fray. And, in typical Trump fashion, he made Turner’s death all about himself while spewing easily verifiable bullshit.

“He founded CNN, sold it, and was personally devastated by the Deal because the new ownership took CNN, his ‘baby,’ and destroyed it. It became woke, and everything that he is not all about,” Trump wrote on Truth Social.

Yes, as we all know, Turner was definitely not “woke.” That is, of course, if you ignore his marriage to Jane Fonda, his call for universal healthcare in America, his advocacy to curb climate change (which included slowing population growth), his creation of Captain Planet, describing abortion opponents as “bozos,” and his founding of the United Nations Foundation.

And let’s not forget that Turner was ahead of the curve when it came to calling out Israel for its violence against Palestinians. “The Israelis ... they’ve got one of the most powerful military machines in the world,” he said in 2002. “The Palestinians have nothing. So who are the terrorists? I would make a case that both sides are involved in terrorism.”

“Maybe the new buyers, wonderful people, will be able to bring it back to its former credibility and glory,” Trump concluded. “Regardless, however, one of the Greats of Broadcast History, and a friend of mine. Whenever I needed him, he was there, always willing to fight for a good cause!”

Yes, Turner was such a good friend of Trump’s, and was there whenever Trump needed him, that he publicly endorsed Hillary Clinton in the 2016 presidential election.

Watch out for my newsletter, ‘Ragebait,’ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: 'A Lawless President': Mehdi Debates Right-Wing Pundit on Trump and the Constitution
item.description: Is Donald Trump upholding the Constitution? That was the question posed to Mehdi and the right-wing Daily Wire’s Michael Knowles at a debate organized and hosted by the Dartmouth Political Union in Hanover, New Hampshire, on Tuesday evening. Held in front of a live audience of Dartmouth students…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Is Donald Trump upholding the Constitution? That was the question posed to Mehdi and the right-wing Daily Wire’s Michael Knowles at a debate organized and hosted by the Dartmouth Political Union in Hanover, New Hampshire, on Tuesday evening.

Held in front of a live audience of Dartmouth students and academics, and livestreamed to tens of thousands more around the world, Mehdi and Knowles, a passionately pro-Trump conservative pundit and podcaster, clashed on this authoritarian president’s record on restricting free speech, detaining and deporting migrants, launching illegal wars, imposing tariffs, and more.

The debate got particularly heated on the issue of the Jan. 6 insurrection and Mehdi’s very specific question to Knowles: did he condemn Trump’s blanket pardoning of Jan. 6 rioters who assaulted and injured police officers at the Capitol? Mehdi may have asked the question half a dozen times… but he never got an answer from an evasive Knowles.

The pair also took questions from students in the audience on everything from the US President’s recent clash with the Pope to whether or not Trump plans to defy the 22nd Amendment and run for president again in 2028.

You can watch their full debate above. Please do consider donating to Zeteo to support independent journalism.

Share


For more Zeteo stories:

item.title: Trump Wants the World to Know He Cares. So Much. So, So Much
item.description: On this day in 1937, the German airship Hindenburg, the largest dirigible ever built, burst into flames over Lakehurst, New Jersey, killing 36 passengers and crew and leaving survivors terribly injured. Thanks to one of the first coast-to-coast radio broadcasts, NBC announcer Herb Morrison’s live…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1937, the German airship Hindenburg, the largest dirigible ever built, burst into flames over Lakehurst, New Jersey, killing 36 passengers and crew and leaving survivors terribly injured. Thanks to one of the first coast-to-coast radio broadcasts, NBC announcer Herb Morrison’s live lament, “Oh, the humanity!” itself went down in history.

Good morning! Andrew here. We’re told Donald Trump’s war in Iran is “a favor to the world.” His administration wants to be “helpful.” Why aren’t more people acting like it? And why does the president appear to be pulling back on his latest “gift” for all, “Project Freedom?”

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ has Trump helped too much? Plus, MAGA collects scalps in Indiana, “border czar” Tom Homan promises more mass deportations, and Abdul El-Sayed and Graham Platner find an AIPAC message they can get behind.

According to Donald Trump and his top administration officials, his disastrous and illegal war with Iran has been one big gift – to Americans, to Iranians, and to people all over the world.

Take Project Freedom, Trump’s plan to have the US guide stranded commercial boats through the Strait of Hormuz, a step necessary because Iran closed the narrow waterway at the start of the war, choking off the flow of oil, sending energy prices soaring, and throttling the global economy.

Trump called Project Freedom “a humanitarian gesture.” And on Tuesday, Secretary of State Marco Rubio called it “a favor to the world,” explaining: “It’s their ships that are stranded. It’s their fuel supplies that are stranded… It’s the fertilizer that they need for their food and crops that’s stranded … not our fertilizer, their fertilizer.”

Yes, the Strait of Hormuz was open before Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu launched their reckless war – and sure, people are now dealing with drastically higher oil prices, and the prospect of shortages of fuel and food.

But let’s keep in mind what Rubio said Tuesday: “We want to be helpful.”

Project Freedom is just one more way in which the US has tried to help people out with this war in Iran – thanklessly, we might add. And why is that?

When the president first announced this war, he told the people of Iran that once the US and Israel were finished bombing, it would be time to “take over your government” and “unleash the prosperous and glorious future that is close within your reach.”

“For many years, you have asked for America’s help, but you never got it,” Trump said. “No president was willing to do what I am willing to do tonight. Now you have a president who is giving you what you want.”

Sure, Iran’s brutal regime remains in power – indeed, more firmly so than before – but Trump wasn’t only trying to help the people of Iran: He was trying to help everyone.

“They would have had a nuclear weapon and, you know, they’re crazy, and they would have used it,” Trump said on March 7. “So, we did the world a favor.”

Yes, this favor has killed thousands of Iranians, and may have also created a scenario in which Iran becomes the sole tollbooth operator for what may be the world’s most important waterway.

According to Trump, the US doesn’t even need the oil meant to transit the Strait of Hormuz. And yet, the US is trying to reopen the strait anyway. As Trump told Americans in his April primetime address, “We are there to help.”

OK, no, the Strait is not yet open yet, and neither Trump’s incessant bluster, genocidal threats, nor retaliatory blockade of Iranian ports and shipping have convinced Tehran to give up its lucrative leverage over the waterway.

Should that really matter, when Trump’s administration comes bearing gifts?

“As a direct gift from the United States to the world, we have established a powerful red, white, and blue dome over the Strait,” Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said Tuesday morning.

Oddly, on Tuesday night, Trump announced on social media that Project Freedom “will be paused for a short period of time.” He said it was being shelved – for now – so the US can see if it can come to a “Complete and Final Agreement with Representatives of Iran.”

“Great Progress has been made,” Trump added, for good measure.

An Iranian official promptly told Drop Site News that Trump’s Truth Social post was “riddled with falsehoods,” adding: “We will not participate in direct negotiations until the United States formally announces the end of the blockade.”

And yet despite it all – despite the success, the gifts, and the favors, not to mention the brilliant statecraft of a new plan every couple days – new research suggests that now only a third of Americans approve of Trump’s handling of gas prices, the chief metric by which the effect of this war is measured.

Don’t they know he’s trying to help?

Death tolls:

  • Iran:Iranian state media on Sundayreported, citing officials, that US-Israeli attacks have killed 3,468 people.
  • Lebanon:Israel has killed more than 2,702 people, including at least 177 children, in Lebanon, according to thecountry’s Health Ministry.
  • US troops:At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.
  • Israel:At least35people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed, according to officials.

And in more war news…

  • Second stage:Secretary of State Marco Rubiosaidthe US had finished initial operations against Iran and was focused on the Strait of Hormuz – an attempt to get around the 60-day limit for action without congressional approval.
  • Closing in?Western media, citing unnamed Pakistani and American sources,reportedthe US believes it is closing in on a one-page memorandum to end the war.
  • No joke:Hegseth and Joint Chiefs Chair General Dan Caine were asked about speculation Iran might use “kamikaze dolphins.” Caine referenced an Austin Powers gag about “sharks with laser beams.” Hegseth said Iran did not have such animals, but said he would not confirm if the US did. Laughter was scarce.
  • UAE hit:The United Arab Emiratessaidit had been targeted by Iranian missiles and drones for a second day running yesterday. Iran denied it.
  • Unholy exchange:After Pope Leo rejected Trump’s latest attack over the Iran War, Rubiosaidhe expected a “frank” discussion at the Vatican on Thursday.
  • Lebanon latest:Rubiosaidan Israel-Lebanon peace deal was “imminently achievable,” but Hezbollah remained a “problem.” Israel issued new forced displacement orders for a dozen villages in southern Lebanon, and far-right Israeli Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrichsaidhe had promised his son “more destruction” in Lebanon.

A majority of the Indiana state senators Donald Trump targeted for removal after they opposed his mid-cycle congressional gerrymandering push were defeated in primaries on Tuesday, by opponents endorsed by the president.

Five targeted senators were defeated, one survived, and one was locked in a tight race, NBC News reported. Another senator who opposed redistricting defeated a challenger whom Trump did not endorse.

“Big night for MAGA in Indiana,” US Senator Jim Banks, a Trump ally, said on social media about Trump’s hardcore supporters. “Proud to have helped elect more conservative Republicans to the Indiana State Senate.”

James Bopp, a prominent Indiana Republican attorney who was an ardent proponent of Trump’s stolen election lie in 2020, told the Associated Press loyalty ran deep in the whole Republican Party.

“Republican voters overwhelmingly support Trump,” Bopp said, “and when they find out Trump has endorsed a particular Senate candidate, they swing their support behind them.”

Trump sought to have Indiana follow Texas in redistricting to gain safe Republican seats ahead of the midterm elections, in which polling suggests the GOP will lose the US House. But the eight Republican Indiana state senators joined with Democrats to oppose and defeat the push. Trump’s fury brought a spotlight to the normally sleepy GOP primary — and big spending, with around $12 million splashed on ads.

On Tuesday, the one Trump-targeted senator who certainly survived, Greg Goode of Terre Haute, told supporters: “Folks here in Western Indiana, we made it about the district and at the same time blocked out all the noise and all of the drama while we did the work.”

As quoted by Indiana Public Media, Goode said he would address redistricting again if it returned to the agenda.

“I will replicate what I did on this last vote,” Goode said. “I will go directly to my constituents. I will do another town hall, and I’ll continue to take soundings on it. I will say, I hope it does not come up.”

Goode also said he “voted for Donald Trump [in] 2016, 2020, and 2024. He’s the only presidential candidate I’ve ever donated to on so many issues. Most issues I find agreement.

Never one to forget a slight, however, Trump has given the senator a nickname that may stick: “No Goode.”

  • Strictly ballroom:Senate Republicanswant$1 billion in taxpayers’ money for Donald Trump’s ballroom – despite press secretary Karoline Leavittpromisingthat not even “a dime” for the project would come from the American people.
  • Studies blocked:FDA officials haveblockedpublication of studies supporting the safety of vaccines against COVID-19 and shingles, theNew York Timesreported. The studies cost millions of dollars in, yes, taxpayer funds.
  • ‘Mass deportations’:White House border czar Tom Homantoldan audience in Phoenix: “You ain’t seen shit yet… mass deportations are coming.”
  • JB jab:Speaking toPolitico, Illinois Governor and presumed presidential hopeful JB Pritzkersaidof Trump: “I think the man has dementia. I don’t really understand how it works up there for him.”
  • Bigotry watch:At the White House, Trumpburbledto a child about transgender weightlifters. At the Education Department, Smith College in Massachusetts, a women’s school,facesa civil rights investigation for allowing transgender students to enroll. The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission issuingtheNew York Timesfor passing over a white man.
  • AIPAC honor:After the pro-Israel lobby labeled their views “a direct threat to the US-Israel relationship,” progressive Michigan Senate candidateAbdul El-Sayedresponded, “I endorse this message,” while presumptive Maine Democratic Senate nominee Graham Platnersaidhe hopes to appear in “many AIPAC fundraising emails to come.”
  • Case dismissed:Reporter andself-proclaimedfree speech defender Matt Taibbi saw his defamation lawsuit against Eoin Higgins, author of, and publisher Bold Type Books,Owned: How Tech Billionaires Bought the Loudest Voices on the Leftdismissedby a judge in New York.

Pete Hegseth says Iran does not have ‘kamikaze dolphins’ in the Strait of Hormuz, but the US certainly does train dolphins to detect mines and counter enemy divers. What other animal does the US military train for submarine work?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

Pearson, a member of the “Tennessee Three“ who led protests for gun control in the Tennessee state capitol after the 2023 Covenant School shooting, will join Zeteo after leading a nine-mile walk in protest of the GOP attack on Black political power.

Hear from Pearson LIVE, as he takes part in the special session Republican Governor Bill Lee arranged to expeditiously dilute Black political power in the South.

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:An Israeli airstrike and tank shelling killed at least three Palestinians, including a child, authoritiessaid.

  • 🇺🇦🇷🇺

    Strike exchange:Russian drones and missileskilledmore than 20 in Ukraine ahead of ceasefires announced this week. A Ukrainian missilehita Russian military facility a thousand miles inside the country, killing two.
  • 🇿🇲 Quid pro quo:Zambiaaccusedthe US of conditioning a $2 billion healthcare aid package on access to the African country’s rich mineral deposits.

  • 🇬🇧

    Activists convicted:In the UK, four members of the banned group Palestine Action werefound guiltyof criminal damage over a 2024 raid on a factory operated by an Israeli defense firm, with one defendant also convicted of striking a police officer with a sledgehammer. Two defendants were acquitted.

This chart from Democratic data scientist David Shor is not good for the Trump administration. Not good at all. As Shor put it: “Just to emphasize how bad of a hole the Trump administration is in right now, gas prices [approval rating] this morning literally plummeted so far we had to fix the Y axis of this chart.”


It’s almost impossible to pick the WTF-est moment from Donald Trump’s White House children’s fitness event on Tuesday. Teaching kids to putt, missing every one, then giving up? Teaching kids the “Trump dance”? Both are contenders, but we’ll settle for Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. lamenting that “70% of adults are obese or overweight,” while his obese or at least overweight president fights sleep beneath him.
